data.title: FNB outlines Q2 guidance with projected net interest income of $325M–$335M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Vince Delie highlighted a net income of $116.5 million or $0.32 per share for the quarter. Tangible book value per share increased 12% to $10.83, supported by strong CET1 at 10.7% and a tangible common equity ratio of 8.4%. These record capital levels underscore the company’s ability to manage through economic uncertainty.
  • Delie emphasized the acquisition of a boutique investment banking firm to bolster capital markets capabilities, contributing to the company’s strategic direction of revenue diversification and enhancing client services.
  • F.N.B. launched automated direct deposit switching capabilities, enhancing their digital banking platform to improve client acquisition and engagement.
  • CFO Vince Calabrese reported that total loans and leases reached $34.2 billion, representing a 3.5% annualized growth. Deposits grew by $132 million quarter-over-quarter, with the loan-to-deposit ratio increasing to 91.9%.
  • Non-interest income reached $87.8 million, driven by strong performance in wealth management and capital markets.

Outlook

  • F.N.B. projects Q2 net interest income between $325 million and $335 million and full-year net interest income guidance remains between $1.345 billion and $1.385 billion.
  • Non-interest income for Q2 is expected between $85 million and $90 million, with the full-year target between $350 million and $370 million.
  • Total loans and deposits are projected to grow mid-single digits year-over-year by the end of 2025.
  • The company anticipates full-year non-interest expenses to range between $965 million and $985 million, reflecting continued investments in risk management and digital initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue for the quarter was $411.2 million, with net interest income contributing nearly $324 million. The net interest margin stood steady at 3.03%, improving to 3.08% in March.
  • Consumer loan growth was led by residential mortgages, contributing $224 million, while commercial loans and leases grew by $72 million.
  • Deposit costs fell to 1.95%, a 13-basis-point decline from Q4 2024, reflecting effective deposit pricing strategies.
  • Wealth management revenues reached a record $21.2 million, up 8.4% year-over-year, while capital markets income was slightly impacted by lower commercial activity.
  • Non-interest expenses came in at $246.8 million, lower than Q4, with efficiency ratio at 58.5%.

Q&A

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Asked about swap maturity impacts on NII. CFO Calabrese explained a $6 million drag in Q2, reducing quarterly impact as swaps roll off.
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Questioned tariff impacts on loan growth. CEO Delie noted softening pipelines but expects demand recovery in H2 2025.
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Inquired about fee income sustainability. Delie detailed diversification efforts, including treasury management and a new investment banking boutique.
  • Frank Schiraldi, Piper Sandler: Discussed CRE portfolio strategy. Chief Credit Officer Gary Guerrieri confirmed a continued focus on reducing CRE exposure to 200% of capital.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a cautious but optimistic tone, focusing on NII resilience and fee income opportunities.
  • Management maintained confidence, emphasizing strong capital levels and preparedness for economic shifts. The tone was consistent with proactive and strategic positioning.
  • Compared to Q4 2024, management’s tone reflected steady confidence, aligning with improved capital metrics and strategic advancements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remained consistent, with no changes to full-year targets despite macroeconomic uncertainties.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards digital banking enhancements and fee income diversification, including the integration of new investment banking capabilities.
  • Analysts’ questions evolved to include tariff impacts and portfolio optimization, reflecting external economic pressures.
  • Management confidence remained stable, supported by record capital levels and strategic investments in technology and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff impacts on manufacturing clients were flagged, with less than 5% of exposure deemed at higher risk. Management is actively monitoring through qualitative allowance adjustments.
  • CRE portfolio risks remain under scrutiny, with reductions in office and construction exposures.
  • Analysts highlighted potential challenges in deposit pricing and non-interest income sustainability.

Final Takeaway

F.N.B. Corporation delivered a solid Q1 performance, marked by record capital levels and strategic advancements in digital banking and fee income diversification. Management reaffirmed full-year guidance, projecting growth in loans, deposits, and net interest income amid economic uncertainties. Strategic initiatives, including the acquisition of an investment banking boutique, position the company for sustained growth and resilience in 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Blackstone to launch private credit fund for the masses in May
data.description:
Blackstone (NYSE:BX) plans to debut its fifth perpetual flagship fund, BMAX, through the registered investor advisor channel on May 1, as it seeks to accelerate its growth in the private credit sector, President and CEO Jon Gray told analysts on Thursday.

"This diversified strategy reflects the evolution of private lending into many different areas beyond non-investment grade corporate loans, and will invest across our credit platform," Gray said during the company's earnings call.

The asset manager calls private credit investing one of its growth engines. On Tuesday, it announced partnerships with Vanguard and Wellington Management to develop multi-asset investment products with an aim to democratize private markets.

"There is a profound expansion underway in the traditional model of providing credit to borrowers, which is creating tremendous structural tailwinds for Blackstone," Gray said. 

The company is now the world's largest third party focused on credit business with $465B across corporate and real estate credit, up more than 2.5x in the past four years, he said.

"One of the most exciting opportunities before us today is an investment grade private credit, where our business grew 35% year-over-year to $107B," he added.

data.title: Trade policy weighs heavily on V.F. Corp with 9th straight weekly loss
data.description:
V.F. Corp (NYSE:VFC), the parent company of The North Face, Timberland, and Vans, is limping into Thursday’s close in the red for a 9th consecutive week as the uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs continues to plague a company with a significant exposure to overseas manufacturing.  While the company has shown progress towards its turnaround plan, V.F. Corp (NYSE:VFC) makes more than half of its merchandise in Vietnam, and another 18% in Indonesia, both still in the crosshairs of U.S. trade policy.

To illustrate V.F. Corp’s (NYSE:VFC) vulnerability to U.S. policy, since President Trump’s inauguration, shares of the apparel company have deteriorated by 60% after gaining 33% in the prior three-month period. The dramatic reversal in the stock comes despite better-than-expected FQ3 results and upbeat guidance. During the company’s earnings call, CEO Bracken Darrell was encouraged by the company’s progress on its transformation plan and set optimistic targets for long-term value creation, including double-digit operating margins.

“Q3 was stronger than we expected. We grew revenue 2% while we significantly improved profitability. The key point to make here is that the actions we've taken so far are delivering results,” Darrell said on the call.

So why hasn’t the stock gotten the message?

Seeking Alpha analyst Mario Silva sums it up in his recent analysis of the company and its problematic stock performance.

“All this strategy set by the management would require time and consistency, particularly when the economic scenario might be the worst enemy for companies like VFC that are in the middle of a turnaround.”

V.F. Corp (VFC) seems to be taking the right strategy by focusing its turnaround efforts on The North Face, Vans, and Timerberland since these three represent more than 80% of the company’s total sales. Unfortunately, as Silva points out, the success of these three brands is not enough to lift the intrinsic value of the whole company. And without a clear strategy to reinvigorate Vans, the brand showed another double-digit drop in sales in the period ended March, making it the biggest losing category within the business.

There are some green shoots to suggest that if the tariff/economic situation improves, V.F. Corp (VFC) could finally gain traction on its turnaround plan. Although Vans is still struggling, sales declines are beginning to soften, the company has cut $455M from total costs and operating expenses in the first nine months of fiscal 25, and its FQ3 net debt-to-assets ratio has dropped to 0.44x from 0.52x a year ago.

“The company has made progress across the key objectives of its restructuring plan, which raises hope that it could continue to see improvements over the forthcoming quarters,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Manika Premsingh. But despite Premsingh’s more upbeat outlook, she maintains a Hold rating on V.F. Corp (VFC) given the persistent challenges the company continues to face.

Wall Street analysts share Premsingh’s cautious outlook towards V.F. Corp (VFC) with a consensus Hold rating and a score of 3.00 to 3.12 out of 5. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating also views the company as a Hold with a slightly better Quant score of 3.24. Based on metrics compiled by Seeking Alpha, V.F Corp (VFC) growth potential gets a D- grade, while its stock valuation gets a grade of C+.

data.title: J&J hands epilepsy candidate to Addex ending decades-long partnership
data.description:
• Swiss biopharma Addex Therapeutics (NASDAQ:ADXN) announced Thursday that Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) has returned all development and commercialization rights linked to its epilepsy candidate ADX71149, ending a more than 20-year-long partnership between the two firms.
  • J&J’s decision comes nearly nine months after the company decided to discontinue the development of ADX71149 (JNJ-40411813) in epilepsy following a mid-stage trial setback for the small molecule therapy. 
  • The positive allosteric modulator (PAM) of metabotropic glutamate receptor-2 was developed as part of a collaboration between the Geneva-based company and J&J’s Janssen unit, launched in 2004.
  • “ADX71149 is a high-quality asset which has completed three Phase 2 studies, so we are excited to regain control of its development from our partner with its high-quality data package and significant material,” CEO Tim Dyer said.

data.title: Senator Warren: Firing Powell will crush the markets
data.description:
The markets will crash if President Donald Trump can just fire the chair of the Federal Reserve, Jerome Powell, said Senator Elizabeth Warren.

During a CNBC interview, the Democratic senator from Massachusetts emphasized the critical importance of Federal Reserve independence, pushing back against President Trump’s public pressure on Chair Jerome Powell to cut interest rates.

Warren asserted that the president has no legal authority to remove Powell before his term ends, stating bluntly, “No. Under no circumstance.”

She warned that political interference would destabilize markets, noting that “if chairman Powell can be fired by the president, it will crash markets in the United States.”

She went on to highlight how financial regulators’ independence — established as far back as the Civil War — protects economic stability from partisan whims.

“We made that bank regulator financially independent of Congress,” she said, referencing historical precedents designed to insulate monetary policy from donor or voter pressures.

Warren argued that even public “jawboning” -- attempting to persuade or pressure through the power of a position of authority -- between presidents and the Fed crosses a line when paired with implicit threats, stressing. “The president should not threaten [Powell’s] job.”

In addition, Warren linked Trump’s tariff policies to broader concerns about executive overreach, accusing him of using trade wars as a “corrupt scheme” favoring allies and donors. She also dismissed assurances that Trump wouldn’t act on his criticisms of Powell, comparing them to broken promises on tariff exemptions.

The markets, however, remain cautiously optimistic, partly due to Powell and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s weekly meetings — a tradition Warren acknowledged but called insufficient against escalating rhetoric.

Warren concluded that preserving the Fed’s independence is not about partisan preferences but systemic integrity: “We need to protect it year after year, decade after decade.”

data.title: Regions financial outlines 2025 growth projections amid stable deposit strategy and market uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regions Financial Corporation (RF) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO John Turner highlighted solid quarterly earnings of $465 million, with EPS of $0.51 and adjusted EPS of $0.54. The company delivered pre-tax, pre-provisioned income of $745 million, marking a 21% year-over-year increase. He emphasized the company's strategic focus on soundness, profitability, and growth, supported by a strong capital position and robust organic capital generation.
  • Turner noted the benefits of Regions' footprint in high-growth markets, resulting in a low-cost and granular core deposit base. He mentioned that despite increased unemployment forecasts and GDP slowdown expectations, the company does not foresee a recession in its base case for 2025.
  • CFO David Turner reported that average loans remained stable quarter-over-quarter, while ending loans declined by 1%. He attributed this to customers delaying investments amid economic uncertainty. Average deposit balances grew 1%, with ending balances increasing 3%, driven by seasonal tax trends and customer preferences for liquidity.
  • Turner disclosed that net interest income declined 3% due to lower loan balances and less origination fee activity but expects a 3% growth in Q2 2025 as non-recurring items subside. Full-year net interest income is projected to grow between 1% and 4%.

Outlook

  • Management expects average loans for the full year 2025 to remain stable compared to 2024, with modest growth in consumer deposits offset by corporate clients deploying excess liquidity.
  • Net interest income for Q2 2025 is expected to grow approximately 3%, with full-year growth projected between 1% and 4%.
  • Adjusted non-interest income for the full year 2025 is anticipated to grow between 1% and 3%, factoring in market volatility and uncertainty impacting capital markets performance.
  • Adjusted non-interest expenses are forecast to be flat to up approximately 2%, reflecting prudent cost management and ongoing investments in talent and technology.

Financial Results

  • The company recorded $124 million in provision expense, aligning with net charge-offs. The allowance for credit losses ratio increased slightly to 1.81%.
  • Non-performing loans as a percentage of total loans decreased to 0.88%, while criticized loans in the business services category rose by 4%.
  • Ending Tier-1 common equity was at 10.8%, and capital deployment included $242 million in share repurchases and $226 million in dividends.
  • Treasury and wealth management revenues achieved record levels, while capital markets revenue was lower, attributed to subdued M&A and loan syndication activity.

Q&A

  • Scott Siefers, Piper Sandler, asked about customer investment delays. CEO Turner attributed this to ongoing volatility and uncertainty, emphasizing the need for clarity on tariffs and other regulatory impacts.
  • John Pancari, Evercore, inquired about loan growth guidance. Turner noted stable line utilization and emphasized the impact of excess client liquidity on borrowing.
  • Erika Najarian, UBS, sought clarification on the reserve's unemployment assumptions. CFO Turner indicated baseline expectations around 4.2%-4.3% and a weighted average in the high 4% range.
  • Betsy Graseck, Morgan Stanley, asked about charge-offs and provisioning. Management confirmed provisions are aligned with charge-offs and highlighted reserves for identified problem credits.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concern over slow loan growth and market uncertainty, with questions focused on credit quality, provisioning, and customer sentiment.
  • Management maintained a cautious yet optimistic tone, emphasizing stability and strategic investments to navigate uncertain conditions, as evidenced by Turner’s remarks on expected tailwinds in Q2 2025.
  • The tone remained measured during prepared remarks and Q&A, with consistent confidence in the company's strategic direction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2025 EPS of $0.51 increased from $0.48 in Q4 2024. Adjusted EPS also improved.
  • Loan growth expectations were revised lower for 2025 compared to the more optimistic outlook in Q4 2024.
  • Capital markets revenue exhibited continued challenges, aligning with the prior quarter's outlook of subdued activity.
  • Management's tone shifted slightly more cautious due to macroeconomic uncertainties and customer investment delays.

Risks and Concerns

  • Economic uncertainties, including tariff impacts and regulatory changes, were highlighted as key challenges.
  • Elevated charge-offs in the first half of 2025 are expected, driven by specific portfolios such as office, senior housing, and transportation.
  • Management is actively monitoring customer liquidity and market conditions to mitigate risks.

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted Regions Financial's strong capital position, stable deposit growth, and strategic investments in talent and technology as key drivers for navigating economic uncertainties in 2025. While challenges such as elevated charge-offs and market volatility persist, the company's focused approach on growth markets and disciplined cost management underpins its projections for stable to modest growth in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Realty Income set for seven straight sessions in green
data.description:
Shares of Realty Income (NYSE:O) were on track for their seventh straight session of gains on Thursday, as the stock rose more than 2% to $58.53 in afternoon trade. 

The California-based real estate investment trust company gained more than 6% in the preceding six trading sessions. The stock has gained more than 8% so far this year, compared to an over 10% decline in the broader S&P 500 Index. 

O is up 4% over the past one month. The stock closed 0.44% higher on Wednesday, at $57.26. 

Realty Income earlier this month priced a public offering of $600M of 5.125% senior unsecured notes due 2035.

The public offering price for the notes was 98.371% of the principal amount, for an effective semi-annual yield to maturity of 5.337%.

Seeking Alpha authors see the stock as a BUY, while the Quant rating system and sell-side analysts have graded the stock as a HOLD. 

Seeking Alpha analyst and Investing Group Leader, JR Research, noted in a recent analysis that Realty Income's high average tenancy rates and non-discretionary retail clients help mitigate cyclical risks, assuring investors of its defensive profile.

“Despite higher financing costs and slower growth outlook, O's solid financial footing and attractive dividend yield of nearly 6% are highly appealing for income investors,” the analyst added.

data.title: Kinder Morgan maintains full-year profit forecast, bullish view on natural gas demand
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) +2.7% in Thursday's trading after narrowly missing Q1 adjusted earnings expectations but leaving its full-year profit guidance unchanged, as the company remains bullish on natural gas demand while dismissing concerns in the industry over the impact of tariffs on exports of liquefied natural gas.

While Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) said it sees the impact from tariffs adding just ~1% to total project costs, the uncertainty caused by them, along with commodity prices, has made the company "a little bit more conservative" in communicating its FY 2025 outlook for adjusted earnings of $1.27/share, compared to the $1.28 analyst consensus estimate.

"We began efforts to mitigate the potential impact early in the quarter by preordering critical project components, negotiating caps on cost increases, and securing domestic steel and mill capacity for our larger projects, which total two-thirds of our project backlog," CEO Kim Dang said on the earnings conference call.

"For these projects, we have locked in the cost of the finished steel pipe and less than 10% is exposed to tariffs," the CEO said.

Any loss in demand for U.S. LNG in the Chinese market would be more than offset by increased imports by the European Union and Asia, the company also said.

Dang said Kinder Morgan's (KMI) Q1 U.S. natural gas production volumes were the highest on record, as demand grew by 6.8B cf/day, with the U.S. setting a Q1 demand record for the second straight year, and the company's analysis indicates potential demand for U.S. natural gas is projected to grow by 20B-28B cf/day by the end of the decade.

"Of course there has been a lot of attention on tariffs, particularly those on steel, and how they might impact project economics. At this point, we do not believe that the tariffs will have a significant impact on project economics," Dang said on the call.

data.title: Enbridge set for seven straight sessions of gains
data.description:
Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) shares were on track for its seven straight sessions of gains on Thursday, as the stock rose 2.6% to $45.73 in afternoon trade.

The Canada-based pipeline and energy company gained about 7% in the preceding six sessions. The stock has gained 6% so far this year, compared to the over 10% fall in the broader S&P 500 Index.

ENB is up nearly 6% over the past one month. The stock closed 0.7% higher on Wednesday at $44.57.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, ENB has a Hold rating with a score of 3.34 out of 5. The company received an A in the prospect of profitability and momentum, while it got a D- in valuation.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 11 analysts gave ENB a Buy and above. 12 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, and one recommended Strong Sell.

Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish and see the stock as a Buy.

“ENB is a business that's positioned well for the future,” pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis by Kody’s Dividends, adding that the company's business model is highly reliable.

“Despite near-term concerns like tariffs and regulatory headwinds, ENB’s diversified asset base, strong U.S. exposure, and track record of navigating economic and industry downturns position it well for long-term growth,” said another Seeking Alpha analysis by Gen Alpha.

data.title: Regions Financial Q1 earnings top consensus; NII holds steady
data.description:
Regions Financial (NYSE:RF) stock rose 1.4% in Thursday afternoon trading after the regional bank's Q1 earnings beat the Wall Street consensus as its net interest income stayed relatively stable from a year ago. Deposits and loans increased from the prior quarter.

The bank now expects 2025 net interest income to increase 1%-4%, down from its prior range of 2%-5%. That implies NII of $4.87B-$5.01B, compared with the Visible Alpha estimate of $4.94B.

Net interest margin is expected to increase to 3.60% by Q4 2025.

Q2 net interest income is expected to rise at ~3% from Q1 2025 due to the day count, removal of Q1 nonrecurring items, fixed rate asset turnover, and deposit cost management. That implies Q2 2025 NII of $1.23B vs. Visible Alpha estimate of $1.22B.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.54, vs. the average analyst estimate of $0.51, fell from $0.59 in Q4 2024 and rose from $0.44 in last year's Q1.

Q1 net interest income (fully taxable equivalent) of $1.21B decreased from $1.24B in the previous quarter and edged up from $1.20B a year ago.

Net interest margin ("FTE") of 3.52% vs. 3.55% in Q4 and  3.55% in Q1 2024.

Adjusted noninterest income of $615M was flat with the prior quarter and edged up from $613M a year ago.

Adjusted noninterest expense of $1.04B climbed from $1.03B in Q4 and dropped from $1.10B in last year's Q1.

Average deposits of $127.7B vs. $126.5B in Q4. Average loans of $96.1B vs. $96.4B in the prior quarter.

Provision for credit losses was $124M, up from $120M in Q4 and down from $152M in Q1 2024.

Earlier, Regions Financial Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 beats by $0.03, revenue of $1.78B misses by $50M
